academic_notes.anomaly_description: Placed in Group 1 due to valence configuration, but exhibits chemical behavior distinct from alkali metals (gas, high ionization energy).
First ionization energy is lower than Beryllium due to the electron occupying a higher energy 2p subshell, which is shielded by the 2s² core.
First ionization energy is lower than Nitrogen due to electron-electron repulsion in the doubly occupied 2p orbital.
First ionization energy is lower than Magnesium due to orbital shielding: the 3p electron is shielded by the filled 3s orbital.
First ionization energy is lower than Phosphorus due to electron-electron pairing repulsion in the 3p orbital.
Configuration is [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ instead of [Ar] 3d⁴ 4s² because a half-filled d-subshell (d⁵) provides extra stability via exchange energy.
Configuration is [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹ instead of [Ar] 3d⁹ 4s² because a fully filled d-subshell (d¹⁰) provides maximum quantum stability.
Niobium has configuration [Kr] 4d⁴ 5s¹ due to electron-electron interactions and energy level crossings between d and s orbitals.
Molybdenum has configuration [Kr] 4d⁵ 5s¹ to maximize d-subshell exchange energy stability (half-filled d).
Ruthenium has configuration [Kr] 4d⁷ 5s¹ due to electronic correlation effects.
Rhodium has configuration [Kr] 4d⁸ 5s¹ due to electron orbital interaction complexities.
Palladium has configuration [Kr] 4d¹⁰ 5s⁰, completely filling its 4d shell and leaving its 5s orbital vacant due to relative orbital energies.
Silver has configuration [Kr] 4d¹⁰ 5s¹ to achieve the highly stable, fully filled 4d shell configuration.
Platinum has configuration [Xe] 4f¹⁴ 5d⁹ 6s¹ due to relativistic effects that lower the s-d separation energy.
Gold has configuration [Xe] 4f¹⁴ 5d¹⁰ 6s¹ due to strong relativistic stabilization of the 5d shell and contraction of s shells.
